content: Antibiotics are medicines used to prevent and treat bacterial infections. They do not work against viral infections.
Antimicrobial resistance occurs when bacteria change over time and no longer respond to antibiotics.
Using antibiotics when they are not needed accelerates antimicrobial resistance.
Only take antibiotics when prescribed by a qualified healthcare professional.
Antibiotics do not work against viruses such as the common cold or influenza.
Take antibiotics exactly as prescribed and complete the full course unless instructed otherwise by your healthcare provider.
Never share antibiotics with another person.
Do not save antibiotics for future illnesses.
Unnecessary antibiotics can cause allergic reactions, side effects and contribute to antibiotic resistance.
Antimicrobial stewardship promotes appropriate antibiotic prescribing to preserve effectiveness.
Antibiotics should only be used for confirmed or suspected bacterial infections after clinical assessment.
Self-medication with antibiotics is discouraged because it contributes to resistance and inappropriate treatment.
Antibiotics are medicines used to treat bacterial infections. They are not effective against viral infections.
Bacteria are living microorganisms. Some bacteria cause disease, while many are harmless or beneficial.
Viruses are different from bacteria. Antibiotics do not kill viruses.
The common cold is caused by viruses. Antibiotics do not help people recover from a cold.
Influenza is a viral infection. Antibiotics should not be used unless there is a confirmed bacterial infection.
COVID-19 is caused by a virus. Antibiotics do not treat COVID-19 itself.
Only use antibiotics when prescribed by a qualified healthcare professional.
Antibiotics should be prescribed only after appropriate clinical assessment.
Always complete the antibiotic course exactly as instructed by your healthcare provider.
Take antibiotics at the recommended dose and schedule.
Never share antibiotics with another person.
Do not keep leftover antibiotics for future illnesses.
Do not reuse antibiotics without medical advice.
Self-medication with antibiotics increases the risk of inappropriate treatment and antimicrobial resistance.
Every unnecessary use of antibiotics contributes to antimicrobial resistance.
Antibiotics can cause allergic reactions and other side effects. They should only be used when needed.
Antibiotic stewardship promotes responsible antibiotic prescribing and use.
Hospitals should implement antimicrobial stewardship programs to reduce antibiotic misuse.
A correct diagnosis helps determine whether antibiotics are necessary.
Laboratory testing can help identify bacterial infections and guide appropriate antibiotic treatment.
Fever alone does not mean antibiotics are needed. The underlying cause should be evaluated.
Most sore throats are caused by viruses and do not require antibiotics.
Some ear infections improve without antibiotics. A healthcare professional should determine the best treatment.
Most diarrhea is not treated with antibiotics. Treatment depends on the underlying cause.
Most coughs are caused by viral infections and do not require antibiotics.
Acute bronchitis is usually caused by viruses. Antibiotics are generally not recommended.
Some cases of pneumonia are bacterial and may require antibiotics after medical evaluation.
Urinary tract infections are commonly bacterial and may require antibiotics prescribed by a healthcare professional.
Some skin infections are bacterial and require antibiotics, while others do not.
Some dental infections require antibiotics, but dental treatment is often the primary management.
Children should only receive antibiotics when prescribed by a qualified healthcare professional.
Some antibiotics are safe during pregnancy, while others are not. Medical advice is essential.
Older adults may have additional health conditions that influence antibiotic choice.
If you miss a dose of antibiotics, follow the instructions provided by your healthcare professional or pharmacist.
Store antibiotics according to the instructions on the medicine label.
Do not use expired antibiotics.
Dispose of unused antibiotics safely according to local recommendations.
Vaccination helps prevent infections and reduces the need for antibiotics.
Regular hand washing helps prevent infections and reduces antibiotic use.
Safe food handling reduces bacterial infections and helps reduce unnecessary antibiotic use.
Practice good hygiene while traveling to reduce the risk of infection.
Healthy nutrition, exercise, sleep and vaccination help prevent infections.
Taking antibiotics when they are not needed offers no benefit and increases the risk of resistance.
Responsible antibiotic use protects both individuals and communities.
Responsible antibiotic use is an important strategy for addressing antimicrobial resistance in India.
Public awareness is essential to slow antimicrobial resistance.
Ask your healthcare professional why an antibiotic is prescribed and how it should be taken.
Follow the prescribed treatment plan even if symptoms improve, unless your healthcare provider advises otherwise.
Responsible antibiotic use helps preserve these medicines for future generations.
Antimicrobial resistance is recognized as one of the world's major public health challenges.
Antimicrobial resistance occurs when microorganisms evolve and medicines become less effective, making infections harder to treat.
Bacteria can develop resistance to antibiotics through natural evolutionary processes, especially when antibiotics are used inappropriately.
Antimicrobial resistance is one of the major global public health challenges because it reduces the effectiveness of life-saving medicines.
Drug-resistant infections can be more difficult to treat and may require alternative medicines or longer treatment.
Antimicrobial stewardship promotes the appropriate selection, dose, duration and route of antibiotic therapy.
Using antibiotics when they are not needed accelerates the development of antimicrobial resistance.
Taking antibiotics unnecessarily exposes people to possible side effects without providing benefit.
Resistant bacteria can spread between people, communities and healthcare settings.
Protecting antibiotics today helps ensure they remain effective for future generations.
Healthcare facilities play an important role in preventing antibiotic resistance through infection control and responsible prescribing.
Preventing infections reduces the need for antibiotics and slows the development of resistance.
Proper hand hygiene is one of the most effective ways to prevent infections.
Vaccination helps prevent infections and reduces unnecessary antibiotic use.
The correct antibiotic should be chosen based on the infection and clinical evaluation.
Laboratory testing can help identify the bacteria responsible for an infection and guide antibiotic selection.
Broad-spectrum antibiotics should be used carefully because unnecessary use may increase resistance.
When appropriate, narrow-spectrum antibiotics may reduce unnecessary exposure to other bacteria.
Take antibiotics exactly as directed by your healthcare provider.
Taking antibiotics at the recommended intervals helps maintain effective medicine levels.
Do not stop antibiotics without medical advice because treatment decisions depend on the infection and clinical assessment.
Some antibiotics may cause side effects such as nausea, diarrhea or allergic reactions.
Seek medical attention if you experience signs of a serious allergic reaction after taking an antibiotic.
Antibiotics should not be obtained without appropriate medical advice.
Community education is an important strategy to reduce antibiotic misuse.
Medicines prescribed for one person may not be appropriate for another person, even with similar symptoms.
Children require careful medical assessment before antibiotics are prescribed.
Older adults may require individualized antibiotic treatment because of other medical conditions.
Do not routinely carry or use antibiotics for self-treatment while traveling unless advised by a healthcare professional.
Every individual has a role in preserving antibiotic effectiveness through responsible use.
The One Health approach recognizes that human, animal and environmental health are interconnected in addressing antimicrobial resistance.
Responsible antibiotic use in animals is also important to reduce antimicrobial resistance.
Good hygiene, nutrition, adequate sleep and vaccination help reduce infections and the need for antibiotics.
Antibiotics may be used before certain surgeries to help prevent bacterial infections when clinically indicated.
Hospitals should monitor antibiotic prescribing to promote safe and appropriate use.
Monitoring antimicrobial resistance helps guide public health policies and treatment recommendations.
Some bacteria have become resistant to multiple antibiotics, making infections more difficult to treat.
Research into new antibiotics and better diagnostics is important because resistance continues to evolve.
Educating students and communities about antibiotics encourages responsible medicine use.
Ask questions if you are unsure why an antibiotic has been prescribed or how it should be taken.
Responsible antibiotic use protects individual health and helps preserve effective treatments worldwide.
A runny nose is commonly caused by viral infections or allergies. Antibiotics are usually not needed.
Many sinus infections improve without antibiotics. A healthcare professional can determine if antibiotics are appropriate.
Rest, fluids and symptom relief are usually recommended for the common cold because antibiotics do not treat viral infections.
Most people recover from influenza with supportive care. Antibiotics are only used if a bacterial infection develops.
Many fevers are caused by viral infections and do not require antibiotics.
Persistent or severe fever should be evaluated by a qualified healthcare professional to determine the underlying cause.
A cough may continue after a viral infection. Antibiotics are not routinely recommended unless a bacterial infection is diagnosed.
Most sore throats are viral. Some bacterial throat infections require medical evaluation before antibiotics are considered.
Strep throat is caused by bacteria and usually requires confirmation before antibiotic treatment.
Acute bronchitis is most often caused by viruses and antibiotics are generally not recommended.
Pneumonia may be caused by bacteria, viruses or fungi. Appropriate treatment depends on medical evaluation.
Not every ear infection requires antibiotics. Many mild infections improve without them.
Pain during urination may indicate a urinary tract infection that requires medical assessment.
Urinary tract infections are commonly bacterial and may require antibiotics prescribed by a healthcare professional.
Most episodes of diarrhea do not require antibiotics. Treatment depends on the underlying cause.
Vomiting alone does not indicate the need for antibiotics.
Some infected wounds require antibiotics, while others primarily need proper wound care.
Some boils require drainage rather than antibiotics alone. Medical assessment is recommended.
Some acne treatments include antibiotics, but they should only be used under medical supervision.
Tooth pain does not always require antibiotics. Dental treatment is often the most important management.
Dental abscesses require professional dental care. Antibiotics may be used in specific situations.
Many cases of conjunctivitis are viral and do not require antibiotics.
Animal bites require prompt medical evaluation because some may require antibiotics and vaccination.
Not every tick bite requires antibiotics. Treatment depends on risk assessment and medical advice.
Mosquito bites do not require antibiotics unless a secondary bacterial infection develops.
Many skin rashes are caused by viruses and are not treated with antibiotics.
Antibiotics do not treat fungal infections. Antifungal medicines are used when appropriate.
Antibiotics are generally not used to treat parasitic infections.
Malaria is caused by parasites and is treated with antimalarial medicines, not antibiotics.
Dengue is a viral illness. Antibiotics do not treat dengue.
Typhoid fever is a bacterial infection that requires diagnosis and appropriate antibiotic treatment.
Tuberculosis requires a specific combination of antibiotics prescribed for several months under medical supervision.
Some forms of meningitis are bacterial and require urgent antibiotic treatment.
Sepsis is a medical emergency that requires immediate medical care. Treatment may include antibiotics when caused by bacterial infection.
Signs of infection after surgery should be evaluated promptly by a healthcare professional.
Healthcare-associated infections require medical evaluation and appropriate treatment.
Preventing healthcare-associated infections helps reduce unnecessary antibiotic use.
Culture reports help clinicians choose the most appropriate antibiotic whenever possible.
Not every infection requires antibiotics. Treatment depends on the cause of the illness.
If symptoms are severe, persistent or worsening, consult a qualified healthcare professional for evaluation.
Cleaning hands regularly with soap and water or alcohol-based hand rub is one of the most effective ways to prevent infections.
Covering coughs and sneezes helps reduce the spread of respiratory infections.
In certain healthcare and outbreak settings, wearing masks may help reduce transmission of respiratory infections.
Vaccination prevents many bacterial and viral infections, reducing the need for antibiotics.
Influenza vaccination reduces the risk of flu and complications that may lead to unnecessary antibiotic use.
Pneumococcal vaccination helps prevent certain bacterial infections that may require antibiotic treatment.
Proper food preparation and storage reduce the risk of foodborne bacterial infections.
Cooking meat thoroughly helps prevent foodborne bacterial infections.
Access to safe drinking water helps prevent infectious diseases.
Improved sanitation reduces the spread of infectious diseases.
Follow destination-specific health advice and vaccinations before international travel.
Safe food and water practices reduce the risk of travel-related gastrointestinal infections.
One Health recognizes that human, animal and environmental health are interconnected in combating antimicrobial resistance.
Responsible antibiotic use in animals is important to reduce antimicrobial resistance.
Responsible antimicrobial use in agriculture helps preserve antibiotic effectiveness.
Environmental contamination with antimicrobial agents may contribute to antimicrobial resistance.
Healthcare workers play an important role in infection prevention and appropriate antibiotic prescribing.
Effective infection prevention and control programs reduce healthcare-associated infections.
Maintaining clean healthcare environments reduces infection transmission.
Isolation precautions help prevent the spread of certain infectious diseases.
Regular cleaning and disinfection reduce the spread of infectious microorganisms.
Antimicrobial stewardship aims to optimize antibiotic use while minimizing resistance.
Regular review of antibiotic prescriptions helps improve patient care and reduce unnecessary use.
Selecting the appropriate antibiotic improves treatment effectiveness and reduces resistance.
Using the correct antibiotic dose is important for effective treatment.
The duration of antibiotic treatment should follow current clinical recommendations.
Educating patients about antibiotics improves responsible medicine use.
Public awareness campaigns encourage responsible antibiotic use and help combat antimicrobial resistance.
WHO's Global Action Plan on AMR focuses on awareness, surveillance, infection prevention, responsible use and sustainable investment.
India promotes antimicrobial stewardship, surveillance and awareness to address antimicrobial resistance.
Responsible antibiotic use benefits individuals, healthcare systems and society.
Teaching family members about responsible antibiotic use helps reduce misuse in the community.
Health education in schools improves understanding of infection prevention and antibiotic use.
World AMR Awareness Week promotes responsible antimicrobial use across the world.
Healthy eating, regular physical activity, adequate sleep and vaccination reduce infection risk.
Preventing infections is one of the best ways to reduce unnecessary antibiotic use.
Everyone has a role in preserving antibiotics by using them responsibly.
Responsible antibiotic use helps ensure these medicines remain effective for future patients.
Protecting antibiotics today helps safeguard effective treatments for future generations.
Prevent infections, use antibiotics responsibly, complete prescribed treatments and follow healthcare advice to help slow antimicrobial resistance.
Antibiotics are medicines used to treat bacterial infections. They do not treat viral infections.

keywords: why don't antibiotics work for colds
content: Colds are caused by viruses. Antibiotics only work against bacterial infections.
Influenza is caused by a virus. Antibiotics are not effective unless a bacterial infection develops.
COVID-19 is a viral illness. Antibiotics do not treat COVID-19 itself.

keywords: can i buy antibiotics without prescription
content: Antibiotics should only be taken after evaluation and prescription by a qualified healthcare professional.
Take antibiotics exactly as prescribed. Do not stop treatment without consulting your healthcare provider.
If you miss a dose, follow the instructions provided by your healthcare provider or pharmacist.
Never share antibiotics. A medicine prescribed for one person may not be appropriate for another.
Do not save or reuse leftover antibiotics for future illnesses.
Viral infections such as colds and influenza usually do not require antibiotics.
Bacterial infections may require antibiotics depending on the diagnosis made by a healthcare professional.
